title: Chapter 4: The Broken Glasshouse
content: The door was half-consumed by ivy, and I had to put my shoulder into it.

Frost crunched under my boots as I pushed through — a sound like bones cracking, quiet and final. The conservatory swallowed me whole. Moonlight poured through the shattered ceiling in broken rivers, pooling on the stone floor between jagged islands of glass. The air was different here. Colder, wetter, alive with the green shriek of things that refused to die. Rotted wooden frames held panels of clouded glass at wrong angles, and everywhere — everywhere — plants had gone feral. Vines strangling their neighbors. Rose bushes frozen mid-reach, their blooms black and crisp as burned paper. Ferns pressing up through cracks in the flagstones, vivid and obscene against the frost.

I breathed in. Copper and wet earth and something floral, decayed — roses rotting from the inside out. The cold needled through my coat, found the bare skin at my throat, and I let it. I wanted the sting. I wanted to feel something that wasn't the weight of what I'd done.
What I hadn't done.
I took three steps into the glasshouse before I saw him.

Heath Cruz was sitting on the far stone planter, his back against the wall, his hands resting on his knees. Moonlight caught the blood on his knuckles — both hands, dried and dark, split skin ridging across three fingers on his right hand, two on his left. He'd hit the boathouse wall. I'd watched him walk there after the hazing, watched him disappear around the corner of the east building, and I'd done nothing then too. Whatever he'd done to those walls, he'd done it alone.
He didn't look up when I entered. He knew I was there. His jaw was tight, a muscle ticking beneath the skin, and he stared at the broken ceiling as if the sky owed him something.
I stopped. Every instinct I'd been raised with — the posture, the composure, the immaculate restraint — screamed at me to turn around and leave. This was his wreckage. I had no right to it.
But my feet wouldn't move.

So I stood there, and he sat there, and the silence between us was its own architecture. Ten minutes of it. I counted the first five by my pulse, then gave up. The conservatory filled with smaller sounds: the tick of frost settling on glass, the creak of vines adjusting their grip, my own breathing — too loud, too shallow. His breathing — slow, deliberate, like he was keeping something leashed.

I didn't plan to move. My body made the decision without me. I knelt near the closest rose bush — a gnarled, frozen thing, its dead blooms clustered at the ends of thorned canes — and picked up a shard of glass from the floor. The edge was sharp. I could feel it, cold and precise, as I held it between my thumb and forefinger. Then I reached into the bush and began to cut.

Dead head after dead head. Snip. The black roses fell to the frost with tiny papery sounds. I cut them without thinking, without purpose — a strange, compulsive violence, freeing the bush of its dead things. A thorn caught my sleeve. Another scraped my wrist, and I felt the bright pinch of it, watched a bead of blood well up, copper-bright against my skin. I kept cutting.

From the corner of my eye, I saw Heath's head turn. Just slightly. He watched me for a long time, this girl in her wool coat kneeling among dead roses, cutting and cutting like it meant something. Like I could prune the guilt out of myself if I just kept going.
Then he stood.

He crossed the conservatory slowly, glass crunching under his boots, and knelt beside the next bush. He picked up a shard of his own — a long triangle of clouded glass, edge catching the moonlight — and began to cut the dead roses on his side.
We worked in silence. Side by side. Not speaking. Not looking at each other. The sound of our breathing and the soft fall of dead blooms and the whisper of glass through brittle stems.
It wasn't friendship. It was truce.

"You should clean those," I said. My voice sounded wrong in the quiet — too formal, too shaped. I was looking at his hands, at the dried blood cracking across his knuckles as he gripped the glass shard.
"You should mind your business." He didn't look up.
"That's the same thing here."
He stopped cutting. The shard hovered over a blackened bloom. Then he exhaled — not quite a laugh, but close — and kept working.

More silence. More dead roses falling. My wrist stung where the thorn had caught me, and I could smell the iron of his blood mixing with the copper on my own skin, two injuries breathing into the same cold air. The frost crunched when I shifted my weight. Above us, the broken ceiling let the moon through in shattered pieces, and I thought: this is what the truth looks like. Something whole, made useless. Something that used to protect, now open to the sky.
I looked at his hands again. The split skin. The blood.

Then I looked at my own. Immaculate. Manicured. The nails shaped into perfect ovals, the cuticles pushed back, the skin soft and unmarked. My mother's hands, my mother's standards — every finger a statement of care and control and the absolute erasure of labor. Useless hands. Hands that had never hit a wall or held a person or reached for something they actually wanted.
"You hit walls instead of people," I said. "That's restraint."

He stopped. The shard lowered. And for the first time since I'd walked in, Heath Cruz looked at me. Really looked — not the glance of someone marking an obstacle or a threat but the slow, deliberate attention of someone reading a language they'd studied alone. His eyes moved from my face to my hands to the single drop of blood on my wrist, and then back to my face.
"You watched," he said. "Didn't you."
Not a question.

The cold air turned sharper. I felt it on my skin, under my ribs, pressing into the space where my lungs should have been. He knew. Of course he knew. I'd been standing in that corridor like a portrait — decorative, motionless, complicit.
I didn't deny it. Denial was for people with cleaner hands.
"Sloane's family donates two-point-four million a year," I said. The words came out flat, rehearsed, the argument I'd been making to myself for the last eight hours. "What would you have had me do?"
He held my gaze. Something moved behind his eyes — not anger, exactly. Something older and rougher. Something that had been worn smooth by repetition.
"Something," he said. "Anything would have been something."

It hit me like a hand. Not because it was cruel — because it wasn't. Because he said it the way you state a fact, the way my father states facts: without inflection, without violence, with the clean efficiency of a blade. Two words that walked into my chest and closed the door behind them.

I looked away. The dead roses lay between us on the frost, a scatter of black petals, and I thought: he's right. I could have said something. I could have stepped forward. I could have done one single thing that wasn't nothing, and I hadn't, because nothing was safe and nothing was expected and nothing was what a Vance does when the cost of doing anything is too high.

The guilt didn't settle. It had been living in me since the corridor, since I'd turned and walked away, and now it opened like a room I'd been keeping locked. It filled my throat. It made the air taste like pennies.
I didn't cry. Vances don't. But the not-crying hurt more than the crying would have.

I stood up. My knees ached from the cold stone, and my wrist was still bleeding in that thin, bright way — more of a scratch than a wound, but I felt every pulse of it. I walked to the planter where he'd been sitting and sat down on the cold stone. Not next to him. Not far. The distance between us was precise, though I couldn't have said who measured it.

He followed after a moment. Sat on the other end of the planter, and we were two people in a glasshouse full of dead things, and the green things that had survived them. Sprouts pressing up through frost. A vine that had somehow kept its leaves, dark and leathery and stubborn. A single fern, unfurled and impossible, reaching toward the broken ceiling as if it could climb through and escape.
I didn't know I was going to speak until I did.

"My father speaks in statements," I said. "Not conversations. Statements. The house runs on them. Dinner is at seven. Catriona will perform at the benefit. The Vance name requires posture." I could feel the words leaving me like blood from a wound I'd been pressing shut. "I started piano when I was four. By eight, I was performing. By twelve, I could play a concerto without my hands shaking. That was the thing — not the music. The not-shaking. The perfect stillness of my body while I produced something beautiful for other people to claim."
Heath didn't interrupt. He sat with his elbows on his knees and his ruined hands hanging between them, and he listened.

"Every recital, every dinner, every photograph — they built it around me. The posture. The silence. The smile that doesn't reach the eyes because it isn't supposed to. It's architecture." I heard my own voice go thin, then steady again. "You don't live in it. You're arranged in it. Like furniture."

The word hung there. Architecture. Not abuse. Never abuse. That word was for people who got hit, people who got broken, people whose damage was visible. What I had was a blueprint. A design so complete and so precise that I couldn't find the door because I'd been told so many times that there wasn't one.

Heath was quiet for a long time. The conservatory breathed around us — the soft rustle of a leaf adjusting, the distant groan of a frame settling under the weight of its own decay. Cold air moved across the back of my neck, and I shivered, and I didn't try to hide it.
"Then we're both prisoners with good manners," he said.
I laughed.

It came out before I could stop it — short, surprised, real. Not the laugh I'd been trained to produce, the one that sat in my throat like a silver bell, decorous and small. This one was lower, rougher, a sound I barely recognized as my own. It startled me. I pressed my hand to my mouth, but it was already gone, echoing off the cracked glass, swallowed by the dark and the frost.

Heath looked at me. Something shifted in his face — not a smile, but the space before one. The faintest loosening of the jaw, the tiniest softening around the eyes. Then it was gone, and he looked away, and I sat there with my fingers pressed to my lips as if I could push the laugh back in.

We didn't speak again for a long while. We sat in the cold with our hands in our laps, his broken and bleeding, mine clean and manicured and useless, and the silence between us wasn't hostile anymore. It was just quiet. The kind of quiet that exists between people who've admitted something they can't take back, and the admission sits on the table like a glass shard — sharp, translucent, impossible to pretend you didn't see.

When he finally moved, it wasn't toward me. He reached down and picked up the longest glass shard from the floor, the one he'd been using to cut the roses, and pressed its edge to the stone planter beside him. The sound it made was a low, grinding scrape — stone protesting against glass — and I watched his hand drag it in a slow, deliberate line. One inch. Two. Three. A straight mark scored into the ancient stone, pale against the gray.
I stared at it. "What does it mean?"
He set the shard down between us and looked at the mark. His jaw was tight again, but not with anger. Something else. Something careful.
"It means someone was here," he said. "Someone who wasn't nothing."

The words landed in my chest and stayed. Someone who wasn't nothing. I looked at the line — thin, deliberate, carved into stone that would outlast both of us — and I thought about the corridor, and the hazing, and the way I'd stood there like furniture in my father's house. Nothing. I'd been nothing.
But the mark meant someone. Someone was here.

I picked up the shard. The glass was cold and sharp and the edge bit into my fingers as I gripped it, and I didn't care. I pressed it to the stone, an inch from his line, and I dragged it down. The same grinding scrape. The same resistance. The same length. A second line, parallel to the first.
"Two somebodies," I said.
He didn't smile. But something in his shoulders unknotted, just slightly, like a fist opening one finger at a time.

We sat there for another moment, looking at the two lines. Parallel. Not touching. Side by side in the stone. The moonlight filtered through the broken ceiling and caught the scored marks, and they seemed to glow — thin white scars in old gray stone, proof that two people had been here, had sat in the cold and the frost and the dead roses, and had chosen to mark it.

When I stood, he didn't stop me. I set the glass shard down on the planter between us and pulled my coat tighter. The cold was deeper now, settled into my bones, and my wrist had stopped bleeding — just a thin dark line where the thorn had caught me, already scabbing over. My body healing a wound it didn't think was important enough to remember.

"I'll go first," I said. It wasn't a question. Standard precaution. If anyone was watching the east grounds, they'd see one student leaving the conservatory. Ten minutes later, another. Nothing connected. Nothing to report.
He nodded.

I walked toward the door, glass crunching under my boots, frost crackling at the edges of the flagstones. The vines brushed my shoulders as I pushed through, and the ivy clawed at the doorframe like it wanted me to stay. I didn't look back. Not yet.
At the threshold I stopped. The cold hit me full in the face, sharp and clean, and I turned.

The conservatory was a ruin behind me — broken glass and overgrown things and moonlight spilling through a ceiling that could no longer shelter anything. And inside it, on the stone planter, two parallel lines. Heath was still sitting there, his silhouette dark against the silver light, his ruined hands resting on his knees.
The lines in the stone caught the moon. They glowed like small, stubborn wounds.
"Tomorrow?" I said.
He nodded.
I turned and walked into the dark, and I didn't look back again, because I didn't need to. The mark was there. Two somebodies, carved into stone, under glass that had broken open and let the sky in.

This is where it begins — not with love, but with recognition. Two predators, same species, finding each other in the cold. Not trust. Not yet. Something before trust. Something that hurt more because it came with the knowledge of what we'd both survived, and what we'd both failed to do.
I walked back to Blackwood with his blood still copper in the air and my laugh still echoing in my ears, and I didn't feel clean.
I felt seen.
